abstract: This study analyzes visual persuasion in coordinated online gambling promotion on Facebook, examining over 2,300 images from 223 coordinated public groups identified through the Vera AI project. Using a hybrid pipeline of large language models, embeddings, and clustering, the authors construct a typology of persuasive visual drivers and assess how generative AI has transformed gambling promotion. They document an exponential post-volume increase following ChatGPT's November 2022 launch and argue that generative AI intensifies established persuasion strategies while enabling industrial-scale dissemination that exploits regulatory gaps between paid and organic content on Meta platforms.
key_claims: Coordinated Facebook gambling networks deploy a sophisticated, multi-layered system of visual persuasion drivers—aspirational wealth, manufactured trust, FOMO/urgency, cultural localization, gamification, celebrity endorsements, and social-relations exploitation—that work synergistically rather than in isolation.

Meta's asymmetric governance, which regulates paid gambling ads strictly but permits organic gambling content under permissive visibility rules, creates a regulatory grey zone exploited by coordinated networks to disseminate persuasive content at scale.
The launch of ChatGPT (November 2022) coincides with a statistically significant, exponential expansion of posting volume in these gambling networks, with a structural break detected in July 2023.

Generative AI does not replace existing persuasion drivers but intensifies and recombines them through immersive, surreal, hyper-real visual aesthetics and AI-generated characters that can circumvent influencer/celebrity accountability mechanisms.

Algorithmic amplification—not just content moderation—is the central mechanism enabling harmful gambling promotion to proliferate, requiring regulatory focus on amplification infrastructures rather than only post-hoc moderation.
methods: Identification of a network of 223 coordinated Facebook public groups via the Vera AI alerts workflow, which detects coordinated link-sharing behavior (14-second time window, 0.995 edge weight) seeded from accounts that spread Meta third-party fact-checker-flagged content.
Collection of posts and embedded images via the Meta Content Library (MCL) and a custom client-side image-downloader tool, yielding 10,671 posts and 2,323 distinct images (2017–2024).
Use of OpenAI's gpt-4o-2024-08-06 to generate both denotative (literal) and connotative (cultural/affective) descriptions of each image using a structured prompt.

Conversion of descriptions into vector embeddings via OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small, followed by dimensionality reduction (UMAP) and density-based clustering (HDBSCAN) in R, performed separately for denotative (101 clusters) and connotative (51 clusters) descriptions.

Construction of a co-occurrence matrix across 366 cluster combinations, with qualitative content analysis by four coders of 85 combinations (≥6 images) until thematic saturation, producing a typology of persuasive drivers.

Quantitative analysis of monthly post volumes (Jan 2017–Sep 2024) using two-sample tests (t-test, Wilcoxon), interaction-term regression, and structural break detection, with ChatGPT's launch as the intervention point.
Qualitative pre/post-ChatGPT comparison of visual content to identify stylistic markers of AI-generated imagery (e.g., hyper-real lighting, symmetry, improbable symbolic compositions).
findings: Aspirational wealth and hyper-masculine status motifs appear in ~55% of analyzed cluster combinations; transactional 'trust proof' visuals (payment receipts, cash-out screenshots) in ~37%.

Identified drivers include aspirational wealth/hyper-masculine fantasy, manufactured trust via transactional proofs, FOMO/urgency conditioning, gamification with low entry barriers, celebrity endorsements (e.g., Manny Pacquiao), exploitation of social relations, and cultural/linguistic localization (Filipino, Urdu).

The Urdu-language cluster employs emotionally charged depictions of women in distress, family conflict, and traditional moral narratives to embed gambling within conservative cultural framings—an ideologically inflected promotional strategy.

Mean monthly posts rose from 2,121 (pre-ChatGPT) to 280,952 (post-ChatGPT), a 13,242% increase; median rose from 30 to 800,000. Regression confirmed both an immediate level shift and a steep slope change (p<0.0001), with a structural break in July 2023.

Post-2022 visuals display consistent markers of AI generation (hyper-real lighting, smoothed surfaces, dreamlike saturation, improbable symbolic juxtapositions such as sharks with slot machines) and combine multiple persuasion drivers within single images.
Two emblematic AI-generated posts achieved millions of views (4.3M and 3.3M) and thousands of shares across coordinated groups.
contributions: Provides an empirically grounded typology of visual persuasion drivers used in coordinated organic gambling promotion on Facebook.
Demonstrates how generative AI functions as an accelerant and intensifier of existing persuasion architectures rather than introducing wholly new strategies.

Develops and documents a reproducible mixed-methods pipeline combining VLM-based image description, dual denotative/connotative embeddings, density-based clustering, and human qualitative coding for large-scale visual analysis.
Highlights the regulatory asymmetry in Meta's policies between paid and organic gambling content and identifies algorithmic amplification as a key governance blind spot.
Contributes a reflexive critique of using LLMs both as analytical instruments and as the very technologies enabling the manipulative content under study.
framing: The paper positions itself within debates on platform capitalism, the attention economy, and algorithmic amplification (Tufekci, Gillespie, Zuboff), and within emerging research on coordinated inauthentic behavior, synthetic media, and AI-enabled influence operations (DiResta & Goldstein, Ferrara). It extends gambling-marketing scholarship by linking digital persuasion to generative AI aesthetics and to public-health and regulatory concerns about vulnerable users.
